original_abstract: Solar cells based on alkyl ammonium metal halides offer a compelling blend of high power conversion efficiency and ease of fabrication, positioning them as prime candidates for affordable thin-film photovoltaics. However, their tendency to degrade under sustained sunlight remains a critical hurdle. In this work, we demonstrate that this degradation is quasi-reversible and can be significantly mitigated by optimizing the device's operating environment. Using in operando electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), we investigated lead triiodide perovskite devices featuring methylammonium (MA), formamidinium (FA), and mixed MA(x)FA(1-x) cations. Variable-temperature EIS measurements revealed that the diffusion coefficient for MA ions is substantially higher than for FA ions. Complementary structural analysis via powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) showed that MAPbI3 undergoes a lattice expansion and a tetragonal-to-cubic phase transition at typical operating temperatures. Based on these findings, we propose that the accelerated degradation of MAPbI3 under sunlight is driven by thermally activated fast ion transport coupled with this lattice-expanding phase transition—both of which are triggered by the infrared component of the solar spectrum. By addressing these thermal effects, we demonstrate that device stability and operating lifetimes are dramatically improved when using white-light LEDs, infrared cut-off filters, or active cooling.
humanized_abstract: Solar cells based on alkyl ammonium metal halides combine a number of highly desirable features: a high power conversion efficiency, a simple fabrication process and, thus, a large potential for low cost. As such, they are among the most promising materials for cheap thin film solar cells. However, solar cells based on these materials are prone to strong degradation under light. Here we show that this phenomenon is quasi-reversible and can be strongly reduced by optimizing the operation conditions of solar cells. By analyzing the lead halide perovskite material of the solar cells—i.e., the lead triiodide—by using in operando electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with different complementary electrode materials and cations (methylammonium (MA), formamidinium (FA) and mixed MA(x)FA(1-x)) the diffusion coefficient of the MA ions is proven to be significantly higher than that of the FA ions. In addition, powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) experiments show that MAPbI3 expands its unit cell and transforms from a tetragonal to a cubic structure at temperatures above 50 °C, which is well within the temperature regime that solar cells are operated at. Therefore, the fast ion transport triggered by temperature as well as the structure change of the solar cell material triggered by the infrared part of the solar spectrum are assumed to lead to the accelerated degradation of MAPbI3 under sunlight. All these thermal effects can be strongly reduced when using white-light LEDs in combination with an infrared cut-off filter or by cooling the solar cells under operating conditions, which leads to a strong increase of the operation lifetimes of the solar cells.

In addition, powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) experiments show that MAPbI3 expands its unit cell and transforms from a tetragonal to a cubic structure at temperatures above 50 °C, which is well within the temperature regime that solar cells are operated at.
